source.title: The cerebral signature for pain perception and its modulation

confidence_rationale: Comprehensive review synthesising evidence that the brainstem PAG-RVM descending system is the hub through which cortical states (expectation, attention, emotion) modulate sensory pain. Directly maps onto SD-021's architecture. Confidence reflects the review nature (no new primary data) and the fact that the expectation-specific PAG activation findings reviewed here pre-date the pharmacological confirmation (Eippert 2009), slightly reducing causal certainty.
failure_signatures: If the PAG activations during anticipation reviewed here reflect threat-preparation (defensive arousal) rather than top-down analgesic gating, the descending inhibition framing would be incomplete.
If dlPFC-PAG coupling reflects attention rather than commitment-gated suppression, the mapping to E3 commitment requires revision.

mapping.source_claim_statement: During anticipation of pain, prefrontal cortex activation predicts placebo pain relief and correlates with PAG activity; the brainstem descending modulatory network is the hub through which psychological factors (expectation, attention, emotion, cognition) modulate nociceptive transmission.
mapping.ree_translation: The review frames the brainstem as a 'gating hub' between cortical state and spinal nociceptive relay -- exactly the architecture SD-021 proposes. E3 commitment engages pgACC/rACC (the cortical state), which recruits PAG (the hub), which via RVM reduces z_harm_s PE precision (dorsal horn gate). Tracey & Mantyh identify dlPFC as also participating, consistent with the executive commitment component of E3.
mapping.mapping_caveat: Review synthesises heterogeneous studies without uniform commitment manipulation. Expectation, attention, and cognitive control are conflated in the reviewed studies. SD-021 requires commitment-specific gating; the review supports the general architecture but not the specificity of the commitment trigger.
mapping.source_context: Human neuroimaging review; synthesises placebo, attention, emotion, and cognitive pain modulation studies; no primary data.

confidence_components.notes: High source quality as a landmark review by leading pain neuroscientists. Mapping fidelity good for circuit architecture but reduced because the commitment-specific trigger is not distinguished from expectation/attention generally. Transfer risk low given all-human data. The key finding -- that PAG activity during anticipation correlates with dlPFC and predicts behavioural analgesia -- is directly relevant to the E3-commitment-drives-PAG pathway.
